AMD.reasoning: Agent vote: 0 bullish / 2 bearish / 3 neutral. The 3-of-5 neutral verdict is unanimous on the core thesis — AMD at $354.49 is NOT an entry point in either direction without further confirmation. However, this is not a simple coin-flip neutral: it is a high-conviction 'do not act now' with a bearish lean. Every agent — including the trend-momentum agent that is structurally bullish — agrees that the current price is statistically overextended (RSI 83-86, Z-score 2.60, +52% above 50-SMA, +74.26% 21d ROC). The two bearish agents (mean_reversion and catalyst_news) both point to the same exhaustion signals and add actionable short theses, but both require confirmation that has not yet printed: a reversal candle (mean_reversion) and a post-earnings catalyst resolution (catalyst_news). Macro context is the decisive tie-breaker: swing_macro_context confirms the AMD AI bull thesis is fundamentally intact, but explicitly fails this specific entry on the R/R screen (1.57:1 vs. 2:1 minimum) and applies a hard earnings-binary veto. This veto carries extra weight under the prompt's macro-veto rule because it is not a regime opinion — it is a hard math disqualification. The breakout agent adds a second hard gate: the April 30 breakout above $352.99 was volume-deficient (1.05x vs. 1.5x required), invalidating the breakout signal and leaving the range unresolved. Wiki memory applies two confidence dials: (1) the NVDA lesson (2026-04-30, EMA pullback Fib dip-buy, -$264.65) is a direct analogue — identical RSI 81-87 stretch, identical AI capex thesis, stop triggered with no bullish confirmation candle. This is a same-setup-type failure that mandates a dial-down. (2) AMD trade history shows no open position and no recent loss on this name — no urgency to add, and no stop-out scar, but the system has repeatedly deferred AMD entries due to overextension, suggesting disciplined patience is the right posture. Post-earnings (May 5 after close) resolution and a clean pullback to the $316-$335 zone — which all three neutral agents converge on as their preferred re-entry — would restore 2:1+ R/R and resolve the binary risk before any capital is committed.
AMD.key_conflicts: The central conflict is between the two bearish agents and the three neutrals, but the disagreement is about TIMING and MECHANISM rather than DIRECTION — which is informative. swing_mean_reversion is bearish (fade the extreme) but refuses to enter without a reversal candle on the daily timeframe; the April 30 close was a strong bullish bar near day's high, which actively disqualifies the mean-reversion entry. swing_catalyst_news is bearish (sell-the-news pre-earnings) with a specific entry zone of $355-$358 and a clear thesis (beat-and-raise is priced in, insider selling is a 'don't chase' signal, analyst consensus PT is $296 vs. current $354). These two bearish agents disagree on entry trigger — mean_reversion wants a reversal candle first, catalyst_news is willing to fade at current price into the $355-$358 zone. The three neutral agents (trend_momentum, breakout, macro_context) are not bullish — they are abstaining until conditions improve. swing_trend_momentum is structurally the most bullish signal in the system but is self-vetoing due to the 10 EMA gap (+12%) and earnings binary. The productive synthesis: trend_momentum bullish + mean_reversion bearish on the same name = 'trend extended, reversal risk rising' as specified in the head trader prompt. The NVDA lesson (0% win rate on EMA pullback Fib dip-buy in identical RSI conditions) dials down any temptation to call this a 3-of-5 neutral and lean toward buying a dip pre-earnings. The volume-deficient breakout (1.05x vs 1.5x) removes the last possible bullish entry gate. Net read: the two bearish agents are probably right about direction post-earnings, but the three neutrals are right about timing — wait for confirmation before expressing the view in either direction.
